states.pageAngle: Georgia is a strong early state because the homeowner guidance clearly ties size to bedroom count and explicitly says garbage disposals require a 50 percent larger tank.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Georgia's homeowner guide says the number of bedrooms determines the septic tank size for a single-family residence. It also says the minimum approved tank size is 1,000 gallons and that homes with garbage disposals need a tank that is 50 percent larger.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: Georgia's homeowner guide says a house with a garbage disposal is required to have a septic tank 50 percent larger than the same house without one.
states.permitSummary: Georgia's onsite sewage program routes homeowners through the county health department. The county environmental health office handles site review, permitting, and inspection in practical terms.
permitPathSteps: Start with the county environmental health office instead of assuming a statewide permit path is enough.
Expect a soil analysis before a septic permit is issued.
Use county inspection and approved contractor resources as the operational path after planning.
states.siteEvalSummary: Georgia's homeowner guidance says all properties must have a soil analysis and that site conditions like water table and limiting layer depth affect the usable drainfield area.
ruleHighlights: The guide says the number of bedrooms determines the septic tank size for a single-family residence.
Peak daily water usage is based on 150 gallons per bedroom.
The minimum approved tank size is 1,000 gallons, which the guide says serves a 3- or 4-bedroom house.
A garbage disposal requires a septic tank that is 50 percent larger.
states.localOverrideNote: County health department implementation and site conditions can still change the final system recommendation even when the homeowner guide examples look straightforward.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the county environmental health office that handles onsite sewage permits and soil review for the property.
recordsToRequest: The most recent soil analysis or site review tied to the lot.
Any existing septic permit, as-built sketch, or county repair record if the project is a replacement.
Confirmation of current bedroom count and whether a garbage disposal is installed.
lowEndRiskChecks: A garbage disposal can push Georgia's likely tank band materially higher because the homeowner guide calls for a 50 percent larger tank.
Water table depth, limiting layers, and usable drainfield area can erase a simple low-end replacement assumption.
County process, excavation, and restoration scope often matter more than the tank number alone.
states.permitTimelineNote: County environmental health review and soil analysis come before trusting the quote, so local scheduling often drives the real Georgia timeline.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Georgia's public homeowner guidance is stronger on records and soil analysis than on a statewide transfer-inspection trigger, so buyers should ask for permit and repair records early.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: No single statewide recurring homeowner inspection cadence has been verified from the current Georgia source set, so local contractor or county practice should still be confirmed.
states.specialAreaNote: Garbage disposal is the clearest public statewide wrinkle because Georgia's homeowner guide says it requires a septic tank that is 50 percent larger.
itemsNeedingVerification: Pull the current rule-text citation behind the homeowner guide's disposal upsizing language.
Decide how aggressively the public calculator should use the 150 gallons-per-bedroom design-flow anchor.

states.pageAngle: Pennsylvania is valuable because DEP gives homeowner-facing size language while the actual permit path runs through municipalities and Sewage Enforcement Officers, which creates a strong unique trust angle.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Pennsylvania DEP says septic tank sizes are generally based on the number of bedrooms and uses 900 gallons or larger for a three-bedroom home as the public homeowner example. DEP also makes the local municipal and Sewage Enforcement Officer role very clear.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: DEP's homeowner page advises sparse use of garbage disposals because they place a greater burden on the septic system, but no direct disposal size multiplier has been verified from the current public source set.
states.permitSummary: Pennsylvania's practical permit path is local. DEP points homeowners to the municipality or local agency that administers on-lot sewage rules and to the Sewage Enforcement Officer for permitting and enforcement activities.
permitPathSteps: Find the municipality or local agency responsible for on-lot sewage permitting before assuming the state page is enough.
Work through the local Sewage Enforcement Officer for permits and enforcement steps.
Use DEP's homeowner guidance for maintenance and sizing context, but expect local agency process details to control the next step.
states.siteEvalSummary: DEP emphasizes that septic systems work only if the site has suitable soil conditions. That makes site and field conditions a major reason to keep homeowner estimates wide.
ruleHighlights: DEP says septic tank sizes are generally based on the number of bedrooms in the home.
DEP gives 900 gallons or larger as the homeowner-facing size example for a three-bedroom house.
DEP says garbage disposal use should be sparse because it places a greater burden on the system.
DEP says larger households may need pumping more often than the typical three- to five-year interval.
states.localOverrideNote: Municipal administration and Sewage Enforcement Officer decisions can change how a Pennsylvania homeowner actually moves from estimate to permit.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the municipality or local agency that administers on-lot sewage rules and ask for the Sewage Enforcement Officer handling the property.
recordsToRequest: Any existing permit or as-built drawing tied to the system.
Recent pumping, maintenance, or inspection history if the property already has a septic system.
Local agency or SEO notes that show whether the site already has known field or soil constraints.
lowEndRiskChecks: If the municipality or SEO path is still unclear, the low end is not trustworthy yet.
Poor soil or field constraints can push the project beyond a straightforward conventional replacement.
Excavation and restoration can dominate the replacement number even when DEP's public tank example looks simple.
states.permitTimelineNote: Municipality and Sewage Enforcement Officer availability often drives timing more than a single statewide Pennsylvania calendar.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: A buyer should identify the local Sewage Enforcement Officer and pull permit or repair records early because the municipality controls the practical next step.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: DEP's homeowner guidance says larger households may need pumping more often than the typical three- to five-year interval.
states.specialAreaNote: Pennsylvania's real wrinkle is local administration: the same state estimate can move materially once the municipality or SEO path becomes clear.
itemsNeedingVerification: Find an additional official source that publishes a fuller bedroom-to-capacity table instead of only the three-bedroom example.
Document whether specific municipal programs surface homeowner-facing replacement sequencing that is worth publishing.

states.pageAngle: Connecticut can be genuinely differentiated because DPH uses design sewage flow, potential bedrooms, and code-complying area rules that national generic pages usually fail to explain well.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Connecticut DPH says residential sewage disposal sizing is based on bedroom count, not actual occupant count, and uses 150 gallons per bedroom. The state also says new homes are based on potential bedrooms and that additions can trigger code-complying area review and soil testing.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: No direct public disposal multiplier has been confirmed from the current Connecticut source set.
states.permitSummary: For systems under 5,000 gallons per day, the local director of health or approved agent reviews the site investigation and issues the approval to construct. After construction, inspection, and as-built review, the same local authority issues the permit to discharge.
permitPathSteps: Treat the local director of health or approved agent as the practical permit authority for most residential systems.
Expect a site investigation and soil testing before an approval to construct is granted.
After inspection and as-built documentation, expect a permit to discharge before use.
states.siteEvalSummary: Connecticut's code-complying area rules make room additions and change-in-use scenarios especially important because the available reserve area and soil conditions affect whether a property still works under the public health code.
ruleHighlights: Connecticut says residential sewage disposal systems are based on bedroom count, not the number of people living in the house.
DPH uses 150 gallons per bedroom for residential design sewage flow.
New homes are sized on potential bedrooms, not just currently labeled bedrooms.
Building additions and changes in use can require code-complying area review and soil testing.
states.localOverrideNote: Local health officials and approved agents have a direct role in site review, construction approval, and final discharge permitting, so a Connecticut homeowner should expect strong local implementation.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the local director of health or approved agent because that office controls most residential site review, construction approval, and final discharge permitting.
recordsToRequest: Site investigation and soil-testing records, if they already exist.
Any approval-to-construct, as-built, or permit-to-discharge record for the current system.
Property history showing added bedrooms, additions, or change-in-use that could affect potential-bedroom assumptions.
lowEndRiskChecks: Connecticut uses bedroom and potential-bedroom logic, so a low-occupancy household does not automatically justify the low end.
Weak code-complying area or reserve area can change the practical replacement path fast.
Addition history or change in use can trigger more local review than a buyer expects.
states.permitTimelineNote: Connecticut's residential path usually runs through site investigation, approval to construct, inspection, and then permit to discharge as separate checkpoints.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Any addition, change in use, or potential-bedroom issue can matter more than current occupancy for a Connecticut buyer.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: The current Connecticut source set is strongest on local permitting and site review, not on one simple statewide homeowner inspection cadence.
states.specialAreaNote: Potential bedrooms and code-complying area make additions unusually important in Connecticut compared with national septic pages.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract a homeowner-safe tank sizing bridge from design flow to public gallon recommendations.
Document whether any local health district patterns deserve special publishing caveats.

states.pageAngle: Oregon is a strong organic wedge because the real homeowner story is permit sequencing and site evaluation, not fake tank precision. That creates a page national cost sites usually cannot explain well.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Oregon puts site evaluation before septic permitting. DEQ says a site evaluation does not guarantee approval of any particular septic system type, and homeowners may also need an authorization notice when changing use, increasing sewage flow, or connecting an ADU to an existing system.
states.garbageDisposalPolicy: No direct public disposal multiplier has been verified from the current Oregon source set.
states.permitSummary: Oregon requires a septic permit to install, alter, or repair a system, and the permit is valid for one year after issuance. In most counties, homeowners work with the local septic permitting authority rather than DEQ directly.
permitPathSteps: Start with site evaluation rather than assuming a generic install estimate is enough.
If changing use, increasing sewage flow, or connecting an ADU, check whether an authorization notice applies before the permit step.
After the site and application materials are ready, move into the septic permit process with the local permitting authority.
states.siteEvalSummary: DEQ says a site evaluation reviews soil test pits in both the proposed initial and replacement soil absorption areas. It also says the evaluation does not guarantee that any particular septic system will be approved.
ruleHighlights: A site evaluation reviews soil conditions in both the initial and replacement soil absorption areas.
DEQ says a site evaluation does not guarantee approval of any specific septic system type.
An authorization notice may be required for a change in use, increased sewage flow, or ADU connection to an existing system.
A septic permit is required to install, alter, or repair a system and is valid for one year.
states.localOverrideNote: Oregon's local permitting structure matters because most counties work through local onsite programs even though DEQ sets the statewide program frame.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the local onsite septic permitting authority or county program before trusting any install or replacement number.
recordsToRequest: The most recent site evaluation showing both proposed initial and replacement absorption areas.
Any authorization notice or prior permit tied to an ADU, use change, or increased sewage flow.
Existing permit and repair history if the property already has a septic system.
lowEndRiskChecks: Oregon DEQ says site evaluation does not guarantee approval of any specific system type, so low-end certainty is limited until that step is complete.
ADU connections, use changes, or increased flow can trigger additional authorization or redesign work.
Replacement-area constraints can move the project beyond a simple like-for-like replacement.
states.permitTimelineNote: Oregon puts site evaluation before permit certainty, and the septic permit itself is valid for one year once issued.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Buyers should ask for the most recent site evaluation and any authorization notice tied to an ADU, change in use, or increased sewage flow.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: The current Oregon source set is strongest on site evaluation and permit sequencing rather than a single statewide homeowner pumping cadence.
states.specialAreaNote: ADUs, change in use, and replacement-area constraints are unusually visible in Oregon's official process and can reshape the quote early.
itemsNeedingVerification: Find a homeowner-safe way to map Oregon's site-evaluation-first process into calculator confidence bands.
Document whether Oregon publishes any public-facing residential sizing examples worth surfacing.

states.agencyName: Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services
states.pageAngle: Missouri is stronger than a generic cost page because DHSS explicitly separates state minimum standards, local authority control, and permit triggers in homeowner-facing materials.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Missouri DHSS says OWTS standards cover new systems and major changes to existing systems, and that local authority can be more restrictive than the statewide minimum. The homeowner value here is understanding permit authority, site-and-soil limits, and whether a project counts as a major change.
states.permitSummary: In most counties, the local health department issues OWTS construction permits, but some counties and cities use another local authority or DHSS directly. Missouri also separates smaller domestic systems from larger or industrial systems that move to DNR oversight.
permitPathSteps: Check the local permitting authority first because Missouri uses DHSS, local authority by contract, and local authority by ordinance.
Expect a construction permit before installing or repairing most residential OWTS work.
If the project is in an incorporated area, subdivision, or larger-flow setting, confirm whether another authority or DNR is involved before trusting the estimate.
states.siteEvalSummary: Missouri's own resources say state standards establish minimum size and type requirements based on evaluation of site and soil conditions, so site review is a real driver of both system class and cost.
ruleHighlights: DHSS says site and soil conditions affect proper system function and system type.
Missouri's minimum construction standards apply to new systems and major changes such as replacing a sewage tank or replacing or expanding an absorption field.
A construction permit is required to install or repair an onsite system on residential property under three acres unless an exemption applies.
Local ordinance can be more restrictive than the state minimum, and the permit authority can be the local health department or another agency.
states.localOverrideNote: Missouri homeowners still need the local authority because the same state can route the next step through DHSS, a county health department, or a more restrictive local ordinance.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the local permitting authority because Missouri can route onsite work through the county health department, another local authority, or DHSS depending on location.
recordsToRequest: Existing permit or repair history for the property.
Any local authority guidance showing whether a residential exemption or special acreage rule applies.
Site or soil review documents that show the conditions behind the current system.
lowEndRiskChecks: Do not trust the low end until the actual permitting authority is identified.
Major changes such as replacing the tank or absorption field can pull the project into a fuller permit path.
Local ordinances can be more restrictive than the state minimum.
states.permitTimelineNote: Missouri timing depends on whether DHSS, a contracted local authority, or another local ordinance controls the actual permit path.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Buyers should confirm the actual permitting authority and whether any acreage-based exception or special local rule affected the existing system.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: Missouri's current official source set is much stronger on permit triggers and authority maps than on one statewide recurring homeowner inspection cadence.
states.specialAreaNote: Residential property under three acres is the clearest homeowner-facing trigger in Missouri's public DHSS materials.
itemsNeedingVerification: Pull homeowner-safe tank capacity examples from official Missouri material if available.
Decide how to present acreage-based permit exceptions without oversimplifying local exemptions.

states.agencyName: North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services
states.pageAngle: North Carolina becomes much more useful when framed around its permit ladder rather than fake statewide size precision.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: North Carolina's homeowner path runs through the local health department permit ladder: improvement permit, construction authorization, and then operation permit or certificate of completion after inspection. The state also publishes branch-level resources that show these forms and procedures explicitly.
states.permitSummary: Local health departments are central in North Carolina. The branch's resources and laws point to improvement permits, construction authorizations, and operation permits or certificates of completion after inspection.
permitPathSteps: Start with the local health department for an improvement permit before assuming the site is buildable.
After the site is approved, move into the construction authorization step before installation.
Do not treat the system as complete until inspection leads to an operation permit or certificate of completion, depending on the system.
states.siteEvalSummary: North Carolina's public materials emphasize local department review and show separate paths for more advanced permit options and for larger systems over 3,000 gallons per day.
ruleHighlights: North Carolina's onsite branch publishes templates for Improvement Permit, Construction Authorization, and Operation Permit.
State law says the local health department inspects the system before it is covered or placed into use.
After acceptable installation, the local health department issues an operation permit, or a certificate of completion for certain simpler systems.
Systems over 3,000 gallons per day require state review and professional design.
states.localOverrideNote: North Carolina remains locally executed in practice because the county health department controls the permit ladder and site-based approval.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the county health department because North Carolina's improvement-permit, construction-authorization, and operation-permit ladder is locally administered.
recordsToRequest: Any prior improvement permit, construction authorization, or operation permit for the site.
Existing soil or site review records that explain how the current system was approved.
Documents showing bedroom count, additions, or use changes that may affect permit assumptions.
lowEndRiskChecks: If the site has not cleared the improvement-permit step, the low end is still speculative.
Systems over the simpler residential thresholds can move into more complex state review and professional design.
Construction and operation approval are separate steps, so timing risk can stay hidden until late.
states.permitTimelineNote: North Carolina's improvement permit, construction authorization, and operation permit are separate gates, so timing can slip later than homeowners expect.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Buyers should ask for the improvement permit, construction authorization, and operation permit because missing one often means the approval history is incomplete.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: The current North Carolina source set is strongest on permit staging rather than a simple statewide homeowner pumping cadence.
states.specialAreaNote: Systems over 3,000 gallons per day move into state review and professional design, which is a meaningful line for the public estimator.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract a homeowner-safe residential daily flow bridge from 18E materials.
Decide when to surface AOWE or engineer permit options in public copy without overcomplicating the page.

states.agencyName: New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection
states.pageAngle: New Jersey gets much stronger when framed around maintenance programs, local board of health oversight, and Pinelands-specific system management instead of fake statewide size precision.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: New Jersey's homeowner-safe guidance is more about management and local oversight than a simple statewide sizing table. State and Pinelands materials emphasize periodic inspections, local board of health involvement, and perpetual maintenance contracts for some advanced pretreatment systems.
states.permitSummary: New Jersey uses local boards of health and local wastewater planning structures in practice. Pinelands materials and NJDEP model ordinance language make ongoing maintenance and local oversight unusually important.
permitPathSteps: Identify the local board of health or local acting authority before trusting a generic permit path.
If the property is in the Pinelands or uses an advanced pretreatment device, confirm whether a maintenance contract or additional management requirements apply.
Treat inspection, pump-out, and local management program requirements as part of the cost conversation, not just the install quote.
states.siteEvalSummary: New Jersey's homeowner risk is not only site suitability; in some areas the real cost driver is the long-term management obligation attached to advanced or sensitive-area systems.
ruleHighlights: New Jersey's model ordinance says regular pump-outs are a key means to maintain an individual subsurface sewage disposal system and extend its life.
Pinelands materials say conventional systems are inspected, cleaned, and certified as functional to the local board of health once every three years.
Owners of certain advanced pretreatment devices must maintain a service contract for the life of the system and provide proof to the board of health.
Wastewater management planning materials make septic management programs a local implementation issue, not just a one-time install question.
states.localOverrideNote: New Jersey needs a strong local disclaimer because board of health practice, wastewater management plans, and special-area programs like the Pinelands can materially change the homeowner path.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the local board of health or local acting authority, especially if the property is in the Pinelands or uses an advanced pretreatment device.
recordsToRequest: Recent pumping and inspection history for the current system.
Any maintenance contract or management paperwork tied to an advanced or special-area system.
Local approvals or notices that show whether the system falls under a management program.
lowEndRiskChecks: Pinelands and advanced-system management obligations can make the true cost more than a one-time install or replacement bill.
Ongoing maintenance contract requirements can erase the apparent low end.
Local board of health oversight can materially change the practical project path.
states.permitTimelineNote: New Jersey timing is often shaped by local board of health management obligations rather than by a simple one-time permit clock.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Buyers should request inspection, pump-out, and maintenance-contract records early, especially in the Pinelands or with advanced pretreatment devices.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: Pinelands guidance says conventional systems are inspected and certified once every three years, while some advanced devices must carry perpetual service contracts.
states.specialAreaNote: Pinelands and alternate design systems create a special-area layer that many national septic pages do not explain.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract a cleaner statewide homeowner sizing bridge from official NJDEP material.
Decide how prominently to separate Pinelands-specific rules from statewide guidance on the public page.

states.pageAngle: Washington is a strong trust state because local health jurisdictions issue permits, can be more protective than state code, and the homeowner inspection duties are unusually visible.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Washington's homeowner story is local-health-jurisdiction driven. LHJs issue permits for location, design, installation, and repairs, can adopt rules that are more protective than the statewide code, and homeowners have recurring inspection duties based on system type.
states.permitSummary: Local health jurisdictions permit and manage onsite sewage systems in their counties. They review, approve, and inspect designs, installations, and repairs, while the state reviews local codes and proprietary products.
permitPathSteps: Identify the local health jurisdiction first because that county-level authority issues permits for location, design, installation, and repairs.
Expect the local jurisdiction to review the design, issue the permit, and inspect the work before treating the project as complete.
If the system is advanced or proprietary, confirm the maintenance and inspection obligations before trusting the low end of the estimate.
states.siteEvalSummary: Washington's likely system type is tightly linked to site and soil limits, and the state openly describes multiple system types, from gravity to mound and aerobic treatment.
ruleHighlights: Local health jurisdictions can administer local codes that meet or are more protective than Chapter 246-272A WAC.
Homeowners must have gravity systems inspected at least once every three years and all other systems at least once every year.
Washington publicly explains multiple septic system types, including gravity, mound, and aerobic treatment units.
Recent state rule updates include stronger transfer and management focus for on-site sewage systems.
states.localOverrideNote: Washington is heavily local in practice because the county-level LHJ controls permitting and may apply more protective local requirements.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the local health jurisdiction because county-level LHJs issue permits, inspect work, and may apply rules that are more protective than statewide code.
recordsToRequest: The as-built permit record and any design approval tied to the current system.
Inspection and operation-and-maintenance logs, especially for advanced or proprietary systems.
Pump and repair history that shows whether the current owner followed the required inspection cadence.
lowEndRiskChecks: Advanced systems may carry yearly inspection and maintenance obligations that outlast the initial quote.
County-level LHJs can be more protective than statewide code, which can move the estimate up.
Missing O&M records can signal that the real system condition is less certain than the seller implies.
states.permitTimelineNote: Washington timelines start with the local health jurisdiction because county permitting and inspection schedules control the next step.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: As-built drawings and O&M logs are unusually important in Washington because owner inspection duties are visible in state guidance.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: Washington says gravity systems must be inspected at least every three years and all other systems at least every year.
states.specialAreaNote: Washington's recent rule revisions add stronger transfer and management focus, so ownership-change content is worth tracking closely as the staged effective dates get nearer.
itemsNeedingVerification: Track the staged effective dates for Washington's recent rule revision and decide how much transfer language belongs on the public state page.
Bridge Washington's public system-type guides into safer calculator risk-score copy.

states.pageAngle: Massachusetts is a strong organic state because Title 5 creates real inspection, property-transfer, and addition triggers that national cost pages rarely explain well.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Massachusetts Title 5 makes septic planning unusually tied to inspections, property transfers, additions, and local Board of Health review. The homeowner value is less about fake gallon precision and more about whether inspection or upgrade timing changes the project.
states.permitSummary: Local Boards of Health are the practical authority for most residential Title 5 steps. Inspection reports usually go to the local Board of Health, while MassDEP stays central for the statewide rule and some special approvals.
permitPathSteps: Start with the local Board of Health before assuming a simple install or replacement quote is enough.
If the property is being sold, check whether a Title 5 inspection is required within two years before transfer or within six months after transfer when weather delays it.
If you are adding a bedroom, changing use, or changing the building footprint, confirm whether that triggers a Title 5 inspection or additional review.
states.siteEvalSummary: In Massachusetts, inspection and transfer timing can be as important as the physical system because Title 5 ties compliance to sales, additions, and local Board of Health filings.
ruleHighlights: Title 5 inspections are generally required within two years before a sale, or within six months after transfer when weather prevents inspection at closing.
If the system is pumped annually after inspection, the inspection can stay valid for three years.
Adding a bedroom or other change in use can trigger inspection when a building or occupancy permit is required.
Inspection reports are generally submitted to the local Board of Health, and the buyer must receive a copy.
states.localOverrideNote: Massachusetts homeowners still need the local Board of Health because Title 5 administration, timing questions, and some local conditions are handled locally.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start with the local Board of Health and, if a sale is involved, the Title 5 inspector or inspection paperwork already tied to the property.
recordsToRequest: The most recent Title 5 inspection report.
Pumping receipts if the seller claims the inspection window extends to three years because of annual pumping.
Any Board of Health filings tied to upgrades, additions, or use changes.
lowEndRiskChecks: A missing or failed Title 5 inspection can turn a buyer-intent page into an upgrade conversation immediately.
Bedroom additions or other changes in use can trigger Title 5 review that was not obvious from the listing.
Local Board of Health timing and filing requirements can move both closing risk and replacement cost.
states.permitTimelineNote: Massachusetts Title 5 uses a two-year pre-transfer inspection window or six months after transfer when weather blocks the inspection at closing.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Property transfer, bedroom additions, and changes in use are the clearest Massachusetts triggers that push septic issues to the front.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: If the system is pumped annually after inspection, Massachusetts says the Title 5 inspection can stay valid for three years.
states.specialAreaNote: Title 5 makes buyer timing and Board of Health filings more important than generic tank-size talk in Massachusetts.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract a homeowner-safe residential flow bridge from official Title 5 materials without making the page sound permit-ready.
Document when local upgrade triggers are common enough to show in a public estimate checklist.

states.pageAngle: Florida is commercially attractive because the real homeowner friction is now the permit-authority split between DEP-managed counties and county health departments.
states.ruleSummaryPlainEnglish: Florida's current homeowner path is defined less by a simple statewide tank table and more by who controls permitting. Since January 2, 2025, DEP manages septic permitting in 16 counties, while county health departments still handle permitting and inspections in the others.
states.permitSummary: Florida's onsite sewage program is now split between DEP-managed counties and county health departments outside those counties. The program is built around permitting and inspection, and some owners or contractors can use private providers for inspections.
permitPathSteps: First determine whether the property sits in one of the 16 counties now managed directly by DEP.
If not in a DEP-managed county, contact the local county health department for septic permitting and inspection steps.
For construction, repair, modification, or abandonment work, expect a permit and an inspection before the system is treated as approved.
states.siteEvalSummary: Florida's water-table and drainfield conditions matter, but the public-facing homeowner friction often starts with jurisdiction, permit status, and inspection sequencing.
ruleHighlights: Since January 2, 2025, DEP manages septic permitting in 16 Florida counties.
Outside those 16 counties, local county health departments still handle septic permitting and inspections.
Owners or contractors with owner authorization can use private providers for inspections.
Operating permits apply in counties that use them and are required for ATUs, PBTS, commercial systems, and industrial or manufacturing-zoned systems.
states.localOverrideNote: Florida homeowners must verify the county-level authority first because the same state can route the next step to DEP or to the county health department depending on location.
states.whoToCallFirst: Start by confirming whether the property is in one of the Florida counties now managed by DEP or still handled by the county health department.
recordsToRequest: The existing permit and inspection history for the system.
Jurisdiction confirmation showing whether DEP or the county health department controls the next step.
Any private-provider inspection paperwork if the owner or contractor used that route.
lowEndRiskChecks: If you start with the wrong permitting authority, timeline and quote assumptions can break immediately.
High water, drainfield limits, and repair-versus-modification scope can move a Florida project out of the simple low end.
Abandonment, repair, and modification work still require permit and inspection sequencing before the project is truly complete.
states.permitTimelineNote: In Florida, the first timing question is jurisdiction: DEP-managed county or county health department.
states.buyerInspectionTrigger: Florida buyers should confirm jurisdiction, permit history, and whether operating-permit or inspection obligations apply to the system before closing.
states.maintenanceInspectionNote: Operating permits are required in counties that use them and for ATUs, PBTS, commercial systems, and industrial or manufacturing-zoned systems.
states.specialAreaNote: The 16-county DEP management split is the most important statewide wrinkle to surface before a Florida homeowner trusts the quote path.
itemsNeedingVerification: Extract a homeowner-safe sizing bridge from current DEP and DOH materials.
Decide whether to surface the 16-county DEP list inline or keep it in a collapsible detail block.
